text: The surgeon identifies the pelvic space anatomy and ureter, using a vessel sealer to dissect the uterine attachments. The bladder is mobilized, and the osteoprotonium is dissected. The uterine artery is secured, and the cervix is separated from the vagina to form the vault through calbotomy. The uterus, along with adenexial tissue, is removed. The vault and port sites are sutured.

The surgeon manipulates the bowel out of the pelvis and transsects the anterior leaflet of the broad ligament. The anterior peritoneal reflection of the uterus is opened for skeletonization in the window zone. The bladder is reflected anteriorly using atraumatic forceps, and the infundibulopelvic (IP) ligament is transected while ensuring the ureter is secure. The uterus is elevated and directed posteriorly.

The surgeon reflects the bladder anteriorly, ensuring a distinction of two centimeters below the colpotomizer for vaginal vault closure. The vaginal fornices are opened using a monopolar hook or spatula. The uterus is pulled into the vagina, and the bladder is dissected by pushing the vesical fascia down with scissors and a uterine manipulator. The uterine artery is transected using a bipolar device, and meticulous dissection and hemostasis are performed.

The surgeon performs skeletonization of the uterine vessels in the gray zone using the wand technique, ensuring complete hemostasis. The IP ligament is transected on both sides, and the uterine vessel is carefully dissected under direct vision using bipolar coagulation, maintaining a bloodless field.

The surgeon positions the ports, including a 10mm or 5mm port placed four centimeters medially and above the anterior superior iliac spine. The procedure begins with incisions using a monopolar hook to perform colpotomy and incise the vaginal fornices, utilizing a pure cutting waveform set at sixty watts.

The uterus is pulled into the vagina using a uterine manipulator, ensuring the hemoprotein remains inflated during suturing. The colpotomizer cup is pushed up firmly to maintain its position. The surgeon performs a complete excision and creates the vaginal fold. If the uterus is large, electrical or mechanical morcellation is considered before closing the vaginal vault. Laparoscopic suturing is performed using a delayed absorbable suture with a needle holder.

The surgeon designs the vaginal fold, emphasizing the importance of preventing vault prolapse. The laparoscopic closure of the vaginal cuff is performed, aiming to reduce vaginal wall defects. The ureteric peristalsis is observed to ensure proper function, while attention is given to the urinary bag.
The surgeon lifts the vaginal vault using sutures placed in the mid cuff, performing hemostasis repeatedly to control bleeding. Copious irrigation is applied to the surgical field.
